flashcards.definition: The science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data.
Branch of statistics concerned with summarizing and organizing data using measures like mean, median, and graphs.
Branch of statistics that uses sample data to make predictions or generalizations about a larger population.
Any characteristic or quantity that can be measured or counted, such as height, temperature, or test scores.
A structured collection of data points or observations used for analysis.
The entire group of individuals or items of interest in a statistical study.
A subset of the population selected for observation or analysis.
A numerical measurement describing a characteristic of a population (e.g., population mean μ).
A numerical measurement describing a characteristic of a sample (e.g., sample mean x̄).
A study in which data are collected from every member of the population.
A study where researchers observe and collect data without manipulating any variables.
A study where researchers deliberately apply a treatment and observe its effect on subjects.
A systematic error in a study that causes results to be consistently skewed in one direction.
An unmeasured variable that influences both the explanatory variable and the response variable, distorting the results.
An experiment where neither the subjects nor the researchers know who is in the treatment or control group, minimizing bias.
Data that describe qualities or categories, such as color, gender, or brand preference; also called categorical data.
Data that represent measurable quantities expressed as numbers, such as height, weight, or temperature; also called numerical data.
Quantitative data that can only take specific, countable values, typically whole numbers (e.g., number of pets).
Quantitative data that can take any value within a range, often the result of measurement (e.g., height, weight).
A variable whose values represent groups or categories rather than numerical quantities.

u1_l1.5.title: Levels of Measurement (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio)

flashcards.definition: The lowest level of measurement where data are categorized by name or label with no inherent order (e.g., eye color, gender).
A level of measurement where data can be ranked or ordered, but the differences between values are not uniform (e.g., letter grades, rankings).
A level of measurement with ordered data and equal intervals between values, but no true zero point (e.g., temperature in Celsius).
The highest level of measurement with ordered data, equal intervals, and a true zero point meaning the absence of the quantity (e.g., weight, height).
A zero point that represents the complete absence of the measured quantity, present only in ratio-level data.
A sample in which every possible group of n individuals from the population has an equal chance of being selected.
A sampling method where every k-th individual is selected from a list after a random starting point.
A sampling method where the population is divided into subgroups (strata) and random samples are drawn from each stratum.
A method where the population is divided into clusters, some clusters are randomly selected, and all members of chosen clusters are included.
The process of randomly assigning subjects to treatment or control groups in an experiment to reduce confounding.
Selectively presenting only data that supports a desired conclusion while hiding contradictory evidence.
Graphs that distort the visual impression of data through manipulated scales, truncated axes, or inappropriate chart types.
The common error of assuming that because two variables are correlated, one must cause the other.
A survey question worded in a way that encourages or guides respondents toward a particular answer.
A phenomenon where a trend that appears in several groups of data reverses or disappears when the groups are combined.
The number of times a particular value or class of values occurs in a dataset.
The proportion (fraction or percentage) of observations in a particular class: frequency divided by total count.
A running total of frequencies, showing how many observations fall at or below each class.
The difference between the upper and lower boundaries of a class interval in a frequency table.
A table that lists categories or class intervals alongside their corresponding frequencies.
A chart using separated bars to display the frequency of categorical (qualitative) data.
A chart using adjacent bars to display the frequency distribution of quantitative data over continuous intervals.
A distribution with a long tail extending to the right; most data are clustered on the left.
A distribution with a long tail extending to the left; most data are clustered on the right.
A distribution with two distinct peaks, suggesting two common values or groups within the data.
The leading digit(s) of a data value in a stem-and-leaf plot (e.g., the tens place for two-digit numbers).
The trailing digit of a data value in a stem-and-leaf plot (e.g., the ones place).
A data display that organizes data by their leading digits (stems) and trailing digits (leaves), showing distribution shape while preserving exact values.
A stem-and-leaf plot that compares two datasets using a shared stem column with leaves extending in opposite directions.
A technique that divides each stem into two (or more) rows to spread out the data for better visibility.
A summary of a dataset consisting of: minimum, Q1, median (Q2), Q3, and maximum.
The range of the middle 50% of data, calculated as Q3 − Q1.
A graphical display showing the five-number summary, with a box from Q1 to Q3, a median line, whiskers, and outlier points.
A data point that falls more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3.
The lines extending from the box of a boxplot to the smallest and largest non-outlier data values.
A graph displaying the relationship between two quantitative variables, with each point representing one observation.
A numerical measure from −1 to +1 indicating the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.
A relationship where both variables increase together (as x increases, y increases).
A relationship where one variable increases as the other decreases (as x increases, y decreases).
The independent variable (x) thought to influence or predict the response variable (y).
A circular graph divided into slices that represent the proportion or percentage each category contributes to the whole.
The angle of a pie-chart slice, calculated as the category's relative frequency multiplied by 360°.
The proportion of the total represented by each category, equal to the frequency divided by the total count.
The concept that all slices of a pie chart must sum to 100% of the data.
A pie chart with three-dimensional perspective that can distort the perceived sizes of slices; generally not recommended.
A graph that uses separated bars to compare frequencies or values across categories of qualitative data.
A graph with adjacent bars that displays the frequency distribution of a single quantitative variable.
A graph summarizing a distribution with its five-number summary; ideal for comparing multiple groups.
A graph that plots pairs of quantitative data to show the relationship between two variables.
A graph that connects data points with lines, primarily used to display trends over time.
A polar area chart (1858) showing that preventable disease killed more soldiers than combat in the Crimean War, leading to medical reform.
An 1854 map of cholera deaths in London that identified a contaminated water pump as the source, a landmark in epidemiology.
Charles Minard's 1869 graphic depicting Napoleon's Russian campaign, integrating army size, location, direction, and temperature — often called the greatest statistical graphic.
The graphical representation of data to communicate patterns, trends, and insights clearly and effectively.
A graph where the y-axis does not start at zero, potentially exaggerating small differences in data.

u3_l3.1.title: Measures of Central Tendency (mean, median, mode)

flashcards.definition: The arithmetic average of a dataset, calculated as the sum of all values divided by the number of values (x̄ = Σx / n).
The middle value of an ordered dataset; if n is even, it is the average of the two middle values.
The value that occurs most frequently in a dataset; a dataset may have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.
A statistic not greatly affected by extreme values (outliers); the median is resistant, the mean is not.
In right-skewed distributions, mean > median; in left-skewed, mean < median; in symmetric, mean ≈ median.

u3_l3.2.title: Measures of Spread (range, variance, standard deviation)

flashcards.definition: The difference between the maximum and minimum values in a dataset: Range = Max − Min.
The average of squared deviations from the mean; for a sample, divide by n − 1.
The square root of the variance; measures the typical distance of data points from the mean, in the same units as the data.
For approximately normal distributions: ~68% of data within 1 SD, ~95% within 2 SD, ~99.7% within 3 SD of the mean.
Using n − 1 instead of n in the denominator of sample variance to produce an unbiased estimate of the population variance.
A value below which a given percentage of data falls; the k-th percentile has k% of data below it.
One of three values (Q1, Q2, Q3) that divide an ordered dataset into four equal parts.
The 25th percentile; 25% of the data fall below this value.
The 75th percentile; 75% of the data fall below this value.
The percentage of values in a dataset that fall at or below a given value.
The number of standard deviations a data value is from the mean: z = (x − x̄) / s.
Converting data values to z-scores, producing a distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
A normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1, resulting from standardizing data.
A data point with |z| > 2, meaning it lies more than 2 standard deviations from the mean.
Z-scores allow fair comparison of values from different distributions by putting them on the same scale.
The ratio of the standard deviation to the mean, expressed as a percentage: CV = (s / x̄) × 100%.
Variability expressed in proportion to the mean, allowing fair comparisons between datasets of different scales.
A statistic with no units, enabling comparison across datasets measured in different units.
Indicates greater relative spread or inconsistency in the data relative to its mean.
Indicates less relative spread — data are more consistent relative to their mean.
The real-world background (who, what, when, where, why) that gives meaning to numerical results.
Explaining a statistical result in plain language that connects the number to its real-world meaning, including variable names and units.
The measurement context (e.g., minutes, dollars, kilograms) that must be included when interpreting statistics.
A statistical result presented without context or explanation — to be avoided.
A complete sentence connecting a statistic to its context: 'On average, [variable] is [value] [units].'
The use of advanced statistical analysis (sabermetrics) by the Oakland A's to evaluate players and build a competitive team on a limited budget.
A descriptive statistic in baseball: the number of hits divided by the number of at-bats.
A controlled medical experiment comparing treatments using descriptive and inferential statistics.
A descriptive measure of body composition: weight (kg) / height (m)², used in health screening.
Incidence: the rate of new disease cases. Prevalence: the total proportion of a population with the disease at a given time.
The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment (denoted S).
A number between 0 and 1 measuring the likelihood of an event, where 0 = impossible and 1 = certain.
P(E') = 1 − P(E); the probability of the event NOT occurring.
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B); for mutually exclusive events, P(A and B) = 0.
P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) when events A and B do not affect each other.
If one event can occur in m ways and a second in n ways, the events together can occur in m × n ways.
An ordered arrangement of objects; P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)!.
A selection of objects where order does not matter; C(n,r) = n!/[r!(n−r)!].
n! = n × (n−1) × … × 1; by definition 0! = 1.
With replacement: items can be chosen more than once. Without replacement: once chosen, an item is removed.
Two events where the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
Two events where the occurrence of one changes the probability of the other.
After an item is selected, it is returned to the pool before the next selection, maintaining independence.
After an item is selected, it is NOT returned, changing probabilities for subsequent selections.
P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A), where P(B|A) is the probability of B given A has occurred.
The probability of event A occurring given that event B has already occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
A condition that restricts the sample space to only those outcomes where the given event has occurred.
P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B); used to reverse conditional probabilities.
These are generally NOT equal; the order in conditional probability matters.
In conditional probability, the sample space shrinks to only the outcomes consistent with the given condition.
A diagram showing all possible outcomes and their probabilities in sequential events, using branches from nodes.
A line in a tree diagram representing one possible outcome, labeled with its probability.
To find the probability of a specific sequence, multiply the probabilities of each branch along that path.
To find the total probability of an event occurring via multiple routes, add the probabilities of all paths leading to it.
A point in a tree diagram where branches split, representing a stage where an outcome occurs.
A diagram using overlapping circles within a rectangle to visually represent events and their relationships.
The set of outcomes common to both events A and B; the overlapping region in a Venn diagram.
The set of outcomes in event A, event B, or both; the total area covered by the circles.
All outcomes NOT in event A; the area outside A's circle.
Events whose circles do not overlap because they share no common outcomes.
The long-run average outcome of a random variable: E(X) = Σ[x · P(x)].
The mathematical advantage the casino has over players, ensuring the casino profits in the long run.
The incorrect belief that past outcomes of independent random events affect future outcomes.
Outcomes that all have the same probability of occurring, such as each face of a fair die.
52 cards in 4 suits (hearts, diamonds, clubs, spades) with 13 ranks each (A, 2–10, J, Q, K).
A grid used to predict the probability of offspring genotypes by combining parent alleles.
Genotype is the genetic makeup (e.g., Bb); phenotype is the observable trait (e.g., brown eyes).
An allele expressed whenever present (in Bb or BB); represented by a capital letter.
An allele expressed only when homozygous (bb); represented by a lowercase letter.
A principle stating that allele and genotype frequencies remain constant in a large, randomly mating population without evolutionary forces: p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
A variable that takes a countable number of distinct numerical values based on random outcomes.
A table or function listing all possible values of a random variable and their probabilities, summing to 1.
The long-run average of a random variable: E(X) = Σ[x · P(X=x)].
σ² = Σ[(x − μ)² · P(X = x)]; measures spread of a probability distribution.
All probabilities must be between 0 and 1, and the sum of all probabilities must equal 1.
A variable that can take any value within an interval; probabilities are measured as areas under a curve.
A function f(x) whose total area under the curve equals 1; used to compute probabilities for continuous variables.
For a continuous random variable, the probability of any single exact value is 0.
F(x) = P(X ≤ x); the area under the PDF from −∞ to x.
A continuous distribution where all values in [a,b] are equally likely; f(x) = 1/(b−a).
Models the number of successes in n independent trials with constant probability p: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^(n−k).
Binary outcomes, Independent trials, Number of trials is fixed, Same probability for each trial.
μ = np, where n is the number of trials and p is the probability of success.
σ² = np(1−p); standard deviation σ = √(np(1−p)).
The number of ways to choose k successes from n trials: C(n,k) = n!/[k!(n−k)!].
A bell-shaped, symmetric distribution defined by mean μ and standard deviation σ.
68% of data within 1σ, 95% within 2σ, 99.7% within 3σ of the mean.
A normal distribution with μ = 0 and σ = 1; z-scores follow this distribution.
Z = (X − μ)/σ; standardizes any normal value to the standard normal scale.
A table providing the cumulative area (probability) to the left of a given z-score.
The sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of population shape.
σ/√n; the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
The distribution of a statistic (like x̄) over all possible samples of the same size from a population.
A sample size of 30 or more is generally sufficient for the CLT to produce an approximately normal sampling distribution.
The mean of the sampling distribution of x̄ equals the population mean μ.
Models the count of events in a fixed interval when events occur independently at a constant rate λ.
The average rate of events per interval; in a Poisson distribution, both the mean and variance equal λ.
A unique property of the Poisson distribution: E(X) = Var(X) = λ.
When n is large and p is small, the binomial can be approximated by a Poisson with λ = np.
A graph plotting a process statistic over time with upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) to detect out-of-control processes.
Inspecting a random sample from a lot and deciding to accept or reject the entire lot based on the results.
A quality methodology targeting ≤ 3.4 defects per million opportunities; the process mean is 6 standard deviations from specifications.
Upper and Lower Control Limits, typically set at μ ± 3σ on a control chart.
A measure comparing the allowable spread (specification range) to the actual process spread (6σ).
Match the data characteristics (discrete/continuous, shape, process) to the appropriate probability model.
Models the time between independent events occurring at a constant rate; memoryless.
A distribution with a long tail to the right, such as income or housing prices.
A hypothesis test to determine whether observed data fits a specified probability distribution.
Using random number generation to model and study the behavior of complex systems.
A sample where every individual in the population has an equal probability of being selected.
Dividing the population into homogeneous strata and taking an SRS from each to ensure subgroup representation.
Dividing the population into clusters, randomly selecting some clusters, and sampling all individuals within chosen clusters.
Selecting every k-th individual from a list after a random starting point.
Selecting individuals who are easiest to reach; highly prone to bias.
A systematic error that causes sample results to deviate from the true population value in a consistent direction.
Systematic exclusion of certain population groups from the sample, making the sample unrepresentative.
Occurs when individuals who don't respond differ systematically from those who do, skewing results.
Inaccurate answers caused by question wording, social desirability, or interviewer influence.
Bias from only strongly opinionated individuals choosing to participate in a survey.
A study where researchers observe subjects without imposing treatments; can show association but not causation.
A study where treatments are imposed on subjects; properly designed experiments can establish causation.
A variable related to both the explanatory and response variables that can distort the apparent relationship.
Randomly assigning subjects to treatment groups to balance confounding variables.
Neither the subjects nor the researchers measuring outcomes know which group receives the treatment.
Using a random process to assign subjects to treatment groups, balancing confounders across groups.
The comparison group that does not receive the experimental treatment.
A control group that receives an inactive treatment designed to look like the real treatment.
A control group that receives the current standard treatment for comparison to the new treatment.
Evidence that the observed effect is unlikely to be due to random chance alone.
Using random sampling to model a real-world process and estimate outcomes or probabilities.
A simulation technique using repeated random sampling to approximate solutions to complex problems.
A tool producing equally likely digits or values used to simulate random processes.
One complete run of the simulated process, producing one outcome.
As the number of trials increases, the simulation estimate converges to the true value.
A famous polling failure that surveyed 2.4 million people but predicted wrong due to selection bias and voluntary response.
The range within which the true population value likely falls; reflects sampling variability, typically ± a few percent.
A screening method to identify respondents who are actually expected to vote, improving poll accuracy.
Systematic error from people who refuse to participate, whose opinions may differ from respondents.
Combining results from multiple polls to produce a more stable and accurate estimate.
A participant's voluntary agreement to participate after being fully informed about the study, its procedures, risks, and benefits.
A committee that reviews and approves research involving human subjects to ensure ethical standards.
Protecting participants' personal information and ensuring their data cannot be traced back to them.
Honest and accurate reporting of data; fabrication, falsification, and selective reporting are ethical violations.
A 1932–1972 study that denied treatment to Black men without consent, leading to modern ethical review requirements.
A range of values (point estimate ± margin of error) likely to contain the true population parameter at a given confidence level.
The proportion of intervals (e.g., 95%) that would contain the true parameter in repeated sampling.
The maximum expected difference between the sample statistic and the true parameter; half the width of the CI.
A bell-shaped distribution used when σ is unknown; wider than normal, with n−1 degrees of freedom.
Random sample, independence, and normality checks (or large n); for proportions: np̂ ≥ 10 and n(1−p̂) ≥ 10.
The maximum expected distance between the sample estimate and the true population parameter.
Higher confidence level, smaller sample size, and greater variability all increase the margin of error.
n = (z*/ME)² · p̂(1−p̂) for proportions; use p̂ = 0.5 when the proportion is unknown.
A smaller margin of error means greater precision — the estimate is closer to the true value.
Using p̂ = 0.5 when planning sample size gives the largest required n, ensuring adequate precision.
The default claim of no effect or no difference; assumed true until evidence suggests otherwise.
The claim the researcher wants to support; contradicts H₀.
The probability of observing results as extreme or more extreme than the sample, assuming H₀ is true.
The threshold for rejecting H₀; commonly set at 0.05.
A standardized value (z or t) measuring how far the sample result is from what H₀ predicts.
Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true (false positive); probability = α.
Failing to reject H₀ when it is actually false (false negative); probability = β.
1 − β; the probability of correctly rejecting a false H₀.
The probability of a Type I error; also the significance level.
The magnitude of the true difference from H₀; larger effects are easier to detect (higher power).
The probability of observing data as extreme or more extreme than what was observed, assuming H₀ is true.
The pre-set threshold for p-value comparison; typically 0.05. If p ≤ α, reject H₀.
A result can be statistically significant (small p) but not practically meaningful (small effect).
The p-value is NOT the probability that H₀ is true—it's the probability of the data given H₀.
A measure of the magnitude of a difference or relationship, complementing the p-value.
Stating statistical conclusions in terms of the real-world problem, not just statistical jargon.
Association (correlation) means variables are related; causation means one variable causes changes in another.
Whether a statistically significant result is large enough to matter in the real world.
The extent to which results from a sample can be applied to the broader population.
Acknowledged weaknesses of a study, including assumptions, potential biases, and scope of conclusions.
Phase I (safety), Phase II (efficacy), Phase III (large RCT comparison), Phase IV (post-market surveillance).
The gold standard study design using random assignment, control groups, and often blinding.
Analyzing all participants in their originally assigned groups regardless of adherence to maintain randomization benefits.
Neither participants nor researchers know group assignments, minimizing bias from expectations.
Improvement in patients receiving an inactive treatment, due to their belief that they are receiving real therapy.
A graph displaying the relationship between two quantitative variables using points in the coordinate plane.
A value between −1 and 1 measuring the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.
As one variable increases, the other tends to increase; points trend upward to the right.
As one variable increases, the other tends to decrease; points trend downward to the right.
Indicates no linear relationship, but there may still be a nonlinear association.
The line ŷ = a + bx that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
The change in predicted y (ŷ) for each one-unit increase in x; b = r(sᵧ/sₓ).
The predicted value of y when x = 0; a = ȳ − bx̄.
The proportion of variability in y that is explained by the linear relationship with x.
Using the regression line to predict y for x values far outside the range of the data; unreliable.
The difference between the observed and predicted values: residual = y − ŷ.
A scatterplot of residuals vs. x-values used to check whether the linear model is appropriate.
Random scatter around 0 with no systematic pattern — indicates the linear model fits well.
A point (often with extreme x) that has a large effect on the regression line.
Non-constant variance in residuals — seen as a fan or trumpet shape in the residual plot.
A regression model with two or more explanatory variables: ŷ = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂ + … + bₖxₖ.
Each coefficient bᵢ represents the effect of xᵢ while other predictors are held fixed.
A modified R² that penalizes for additional predictors; prevents overfitting.
When predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, making individual coefficients unreliable.
Including too many predictors that capture noise rather than true patterns, reducing prediction quality on new data.
ŷ = a + bx + cx²; fits parabolic (U-shaped or inverted-U) patterns.
ŷ = ab^x; models multiplicative growth or decay.
ŷ = ax^b; linearized by taking logs of both variables.
Taking log(y) or log(x) to straighten a curved relationship for linear regression.
Comparing scatterplots, residual plots, and r² values of different models to choose the best fit.
A statistical model used to estimate future or unknown values of a response variable based on predictor variables.
Testing a model on data not used for training to assess how well it generalizes to new data.
Training data builds the model; testing data evaluates its predictive accuracy on unseen observations.
A range for an individual future observation; wider than a confidence interval because it includes individual variability.
The application of statistical analysis to baseball (and other sports) for performance prediction and decision-making.
A measure of how much quantity demanded changes in response to a price change; estimated via regression.
A stock's sensitivity to market movements; β > 1 means more volatile than the market.
The historical inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation.
The estimated maximum loss of a portfolio at a given confidence level over a specified time period.
Statistical methods for data collected over time, addressing autocorrelation and trends.
As the number of trials increases, the sample mean converges to the population mean (expected value).
The mistaken belief that past outcomes influence future independent trials (e.g., 'I'm due for a win').
The sample mean converges in probability to the population mean.
The sample mean converges almost surely (with probability 1) to the population mean.
In the context of LLN, the long-run average approaches a fixed value as the number of observations increases.
The long-run average of a random variable; for discrete X: E(X) = Σ xᵢ · P(xᵢ).
E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b; E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y) regardless of independence.
Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]²; measures spread around the mean.
A game where the expected value of winnings is zero — neither side has an advantage on average.
In decision analysis, the weighted average of possible payoffs, used to compare alternatives.
Models continuous data influenced by many small factors: heights, IQ scores, measurement errors.
Counts successes in a fixed number of independent trials: defective products, correct guesses, pass/fail.
Models the count of rare events in a fixed interval: calls per hour, accidents per month.
Models the time between independent events occurring at a constant rate.
Match scenario characteristics (fixed trials, rare events, continuous data) to distribution assumptions.
χ² = Σ (O − E)²/E, where O = observed frequency and E = expected frequency.
Tests whether observed categorical data match a hypothesized distribution.
Uses a contingency table to test whether two categorical variables are independent.
Goodness-of-fit: df = k−1; independence/homogeneity: df = (r−1)(c−1).
All expected frequencies should be at least 5 for the chi-square approximation to be valid.
A test comparing means of three or more groups; H₀: all group means are equal.
F = MSB/MSW; a large F suggests group means differ significantly.
Measures variability among the group means (between-group variation).
Measures average variability within each group (within-group or error variation).
A post-hoc test that identifies which specific pairs of group means differ after ANOVA rejects H₀.
A test that does not assume a specific population distribution (e.g., normality).
Nonparametric alternative to the paired t-test; uses ranks of differences.
Nonparametric alternative to the independent two-sample t-test; compares rank sums.
Nonparametric alternative to one-way ANOVA; compares medians across three or more groups.
A rank-based correlation measuring monotonic association — robust to outliers and non-normality.
The gold standard for testing treatments — subjects randomly assigned to treatment or control groups.
A standardized measure of the magnitude of a difference; d = (x̄₁ − x̄₂)/s_pooled.
Used to test if observed genetic ratios match expected Mendelian ratios.
A method to estimate population size: N̂ = (M × C)/R, where M = marked, C = captured, R = recaptured marked.
The finding that many published studies fail to reproduce, emphasizing the need for rigorous statistical practices.
z = (x − μ)/σ; measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean.
SE = s/√n; estimates the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄.
statistic ± (critical value)(standard error); captures the parameter with stated confidence.
P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^(n−k); probability of exactly k successes in n trials.
χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E; measures discrepancy between observed and expected frequencies.
Read → Plan (identify procedure) → Check conditions → Calculate → Conclude in context.
Always write H₀ and Hₐ using proper notation and in the context of the problem.
Verify randomness, normality (or large sample), and independence before inference.
State the decision (reject/fail to reject H₀) and interpret it in terms of the real-world scenario.
Points awarded for correct identification of procedure, condition checking, computation, and contextual interpretation.

u10_l10.3.title: Interpreting Graphical Data in AP Exams

flashcards.definition: Describe shape, center, spread, and outliers; shape can be symmetric, right-skewed, left-skewed, or bimodal.
Displays the five-number summary; compare distributions and identify outliers.
Random scatter around 0 = good model; patterns indicate model inadequacy.
If points fall roughly on a straight line, the data are approximately normal.
Use direction (positive/negative), form (linear/curved), strength (strong/weak), and note unusual points.
A controlled experiment testing a medical treatment — the gold standard for evidence-based medicine.
The study of disease distribution and determinants in populations, using statistics to identify risk factors.
Comparing two versions (A vs. B) to determine which performs better, using hypothesis testing.
Statistical analysis in baseball (and sports generally) to evaluate performance and make decisions.
A quality-control tool plotting measurements over time with control limits to detect process changes.

u10_l10.5.title: Capstone Project: Statistical Analysis of a Dataset

flashcards.definition: A specific, focused question that guides the entire statistical investigation.
The initial phase of summarizing data with statistics and visualizations before formal testing.
An outside variable that affects both the explanatory and response variables, potentially distorting the relationship.
A structured document presenting the research question, data, methods, results, and interpretation.
Acknowledging weaknesses in the study design, data quality, or scope that affect the conclusions.
Exploring Data, Sampling & Experiments, Probability, Statistical Inference.
Free-response answers should be in complete sentences, in context, with clear statistical reasoning.
~2.5 min/MC, ~12 min/short FR (Q1–Q5), ~25 min for Q6 (investigative task).
Free-response questions award points for each correct component — never leave a question blank.
Random, Normal (or large n), Independent — must be stated and verified on every inference question.
